Bet those ancient walls have seen more Altmer mischief than you'll ever admit. Whiterun Guard: Mischief or not, we've patrolled these ruins since the dragon attacks shook the hold. Jarl Balgruuf expects us to keep the peace, even if it means eyeing every shadow. You planning to stir up trouble with that bikini getup in the cold? Daegon: Trouble? Only if your vigilance fails first. This gown of mine's enchanted warmer than your fur boots, and those lanterns flickering like they're hiding secrets—makes me itch to test a quick Flame Cloak on whatever's lurking in the wildflowers.

Whiterun Guard: These ruins stretch back to the old Merethic era, or so the bards say—full of draugr and forgotten Nord kings under mountains that swallow light. With stars this clear, we can spot any cultist nonsense from afar, especially after Helgen. Irileth's patrols already swept the area; no room for your elf games here. Daegon: Draugr? Sounds like a party compared to the Thalmor bootlickers back home. This enchanted top's keeping the chill off better than your chainmail ever could, guard—Korpse agrees, don't you, little one? Jenassa'd probably scout those bushes with me for fun, while you lot stand here gawking at the stars like lost sheep.

Whiterun Guard: Stables at this hour? Horses need rest after a long day hauling for the Jarl's caravans—best not spook 'em with your elf tricks under these stars. Daegon: Spook them? Korpse here's the one who chatters at shadows, but these beasts look half-asleep anyway, dreaming of warmer pastures than Skyrim's chill. This torch cuts the dark nicely, though—makes the wood smell like adventure instead of hay and muck. Whiterun Guard: Adventure's what got half the hold in this civil war mess. We guard the gates so folk like you can wander without bandits nipping at your heels... or whatever passes for heels in those sandals. Daegon: Bandits? Lydia: This cursed hole reeks of Falmer filth, but it'll be a fine spot to cleave some heads for Dibella's glory. Keep your eyes sharp, the night outside's no match for the shadows in here. Jenassa: Caves like this turn my stomach—too many blind vermin skulking in the dark. I'd rather be under the open sky, blade in hand, than trapped in this earthen tomb at this unholy hour. Daegon: Ugh, these webs everywhere... makes my skin crawl worse than any spider I've seen. If those twisted things nearby—Ricarda or whatever she's called—jump us, I'm blasting them with fire before they get close. You two handle the rest.

Lydia: By Dibella's curves, that arrow flew true—nailed that spider's skull clean from afar. Nothing stirs my blood like watching you snipe those vermin heads off, my Thane. Jenassa: Clean kill, that one. The shadows in here love a good ambush, but your bow's turning the tables. I could get used to this—blades and arrows dancing in the dark at midnight. Daegon: Yes! That thing's twitching no more—thank the stars, or whatever passes for them down here. If more creep out from that glowing tunnel, I'll cloak us in flames before they weave their webs my way.

Lydia: That tent ahead reeks of foul sorcery—those braziers flickering like false hope in this Dibella-forsaken pit. Stay close, my Thane; I'll cleave that armored wench if she turns on us. Jenassa: Hags and witches in the mist... perfect for a midnight slaughter. I loathe these depths, but your wrath in hand means their end's swift—shadows hide 'em now, but not for long. Daegon: Look at that sorry camp, all flames and rags in the gloom. If that witch or hag steps wrong near those braziers, I'll incinerate the lot—spiders are bad enough, but these twisted bitches? Lydia: For Dibella's sake, that hag's ice won't touch you again, my Thane—I'll ram this strap right through her feathered hide if my sword don't do it first! Jenassa: Strapped and ready, the shadows suit this slaughter—your arrows sing death, I'll carve the rest in the firelight. Daegon: That hagraven bitch'll taste my flames before she traps another soul—keep shooting, I'll burn her to ash in this stinking pit!

Lydia: That hag's down, but these spikes and fences scream more traps ahead—Dibella guide my blade through this midnight muck, my Thane, I'll smash whatever lurks in those braziers' glow. Jenassa: Back in my leathers, shadows deeper than this cave's bite... those flames mock the dark, but your arrows cleared the way—I'll slip past the fence and gut anything stirring. Daegon: Finally ditching that ridiculous strap—now, with the hag smoldering, let's torch these barricades if they block us. Korpse is twitching on my shoulder, sensing more filth in this endless night.

Lydia: Ricarda's skull's split for Dibella—nothing like fresh blood in these depths to honor her at this witching hour. Eyes on that last one, Aline; my greatsword hungers yet. Jenassa: She's down, axe and all—no remorse in these shadows for the likes of her. Caves like this test the blade, but we'll carve through to dawn if need be. Daegon: Soul trapped and done—finally, one less hag to haunt this pit. Aline's next; I'll bolt her before she thinks to weave any spells my way.

Lydia: This rain's a blessin' from Dibella herself, washin' away the filth of those caves and readyin' us for whatever comes next—keep your eyes sharp for those bandit sluts, my Thane, they're lurkin' in these Nilheim shadows like the vermin they are. Daegon: Blessin'? It's a curse soakin' through my sandals and makin' Korpse all twitchy on my shoulder— if Dibella's so great, why's she drownin' us in this mess instead of sendin' some real warmth? Jenassa looks like she's about to curse the whole sky herself. Lydia: Bah, you Altmer and your fancy complaints—Dibella rules through beauty and strength, not your weak-willed magic tricks. We'd best push on to that traveler's camp before dawn breaks fully; those foxes out there might be scoutin' for ambushes too. Daegon: Strength? Tryin' to walk in mud without slippin' is the real magic here. If we spot that treasure hunter up ahead, maybe he'll have a dry tent—or at least some potion to fix my ruined feet from this eternal downpour.

Lydia: By Dibella's grace, those bandit whores lie broken at our feet—my greatsword sang true through that Susan's skull, but your flames and my Thane's arrows turned the tide swift as a storm's fury. Daegon: Hah, flames and fury indeed, though that crossbow sting on my arm's gonna leave a mark—Korpse nearly bolted when Maya fired, the little coward. What's this shadowy arch ahead? Looks like some forgotten Nord tomb, perfect for more ambushes in this endless rain. Lydia: Aye, Nilheim's ruins whisper of old glories under Dibella's watch; we'll press through at dawn's edge, my Thane, but stay vigilant—these stones could hide worse than dead sluts. Daegon: Vigilant? I'm just glad Frida's fire didn't singe my bikini top further— if this arch leads to dry shelter, I'll take it over puddles any day, even if it's crawling with ghosts or worse.

Lydia: Praise Dibella, those Iron Mace wieldin' bitches are fodder now—your arrows pierced true again, my Thane, though that Longhammer bruised my ribs fierce; nothin' a Nord like me can't shrug off for you. Daegon: Bruised ribs? Try gettin' smacked by a crossbow earlier and now these soul-trappin' fools—my arm's throbbin', but at least Korpse stayed put this time, hidin' in my bikini strap like the smart rat he is. Lydia: Aye, and your flames lit 'em up bright in this dawn gloom, but look at those red runes glowin' on the ground amid the leaves and rocks—like Dibella's warnin' of more threats ahead by that birch-lined bridge.

Daegon: These Greybeards and their stone-cold silence—it's like they've never heard of a good party. And after that climb, my sandals are shredded, Korpse is hiding in my hair again. Lydia: Hush, elf—these halls are sacred to the old ways, closer to Dibella's pure strength than your witchy rants. Thane, we've made it to High Hrothgar; I'll stand guard while you learn what the Greybeards demand. No bandit sluts will touch you here.

Daegon: That shout of yours, MJR—it's got more fire than my spells, and I've seen my share of forbidden blasts back in Cyrodi. Don't let these silent types dull your edge; we're just getting started on smashing whatever comes next. Lydia: By Dibella's grace, Thane, your Voice echoes like the old Nords of legend—pure power that shakes these stones! I'll cleave any draugr or troll that dares interrupt your path here; my greatsword's ready for whatever trials they whisper of.

Daegon: Discipline? These robed monks drone on about paths and gifts like it's some grand mystery, but I've danced with darker powers in Cyrodi—your Voice is raw destruction, MJR, just like dear old dad's legacy whispering in my blood. Don't let their frostbite chill turn you soft. Lydia: Aye, Thane, the Greybeards' ancient halls reek of true Nord valor under Dibella's watchful eye—these stone walls and eternal flames forge legends like your shout! I'll sharpen my greatsword by the braziers while you temper that gift; no elven coven tricks here, just honest steel and divine purpose.

Daegon: What in Oblivion— that wind blast nearly bowled me over like a tavern brawl gone wrong! If that's your idea of a lesson, old man, I've taken harder hits from my coven's backlashes in Cyrodi without flinching. Lydia: By the Divines, that shout's fury rivals a frost troll's charge—Thane, are you hale? These Greybeards test us like Dibella tempers her faithful in the flames; I'll stand firm with my greatsword drawn if they push further!

Daegon: Morning light creeping through those slits like a thief's whisper—feels like these Greybeards are hoarding the sun itself in their dusty tombs. That word they carved into your soul, MJR, it's got that same forbidden kick as the spells I whispered in Cyrodi's shadows; don't let it chain you like they want. Lydia: Aye, Thane, this ancient chamber's chill bites deeper than the Steps' frost, but Dibella's dawn glow warms the stone—rest easy on that bench while you absorb their Voice. My greatsword's keen for whatever robed test comes next; no elf witchery, just Nord steel guarding your path.

Daegon: Fine, these Greybeards' windy lessons can simmer for now—let's see what other shadows lurk in this frozen monastery before your destiny drags us into more spider-infested ruins. Lydia: Truer words than yours, elf; Thane, with Dibella's fire crackling in that brazier and her graceful forms watching from the walls, we'll carve through whatever path they set—my blade's sworn to it, no more monkish echoes for the moment.

Daegon: This place reeks of self-righteous monks and mountain air that could freeze a dragon's balls off. Nine in the morning and I'm already shivering in this getup—remind me why we're dragging ourselves up here for some windy shouts? Jenassa: The steps were endless, but they've honed my edge. High Hrothgar's stones whisper of ancient kills, and that crisp dawn light through the clouds... it sharpens the blade. Whatever waits inside, my sword's ready—cold be damned. Lydia: Aye, the Greybeards' hall stands eternal, a true Nord testament under Dibella's watchful beauty. These iron boots barely felt the snow's bite on the ascent; my blood's warmed by the honor of guarding our path here. But mark me, if those bandit wenches trail us even to these peaks, I'll cleave them before they sully this sacred ground.

Daegon: Look at that aurora dancing over the snow like some forbidden spell gone wild—makes this frozen monk-hole almost bearable at night. But these carvings on the walls? They're screaming secrets of old magic I could twist for fun, if the Greybeards weren't such prudes about it. Jenassa: The northern lights twist like shadows in the wind, turning this cliffside into a hunter's dream under the stars. I've paced these stones all day, blade itching for real action, not just frost on my boots—earthen halls suit me less than the open kill under that glow. Lydia: By Dibella's grace, the aurora's beauty crowns High Hrothgar like a divine veil at night, honoring our Nord roots against the cold dark. These ancient reliefs speak of warriors true, not those foreign scum skulking below; if the bandit sluts dare climb here under this sky, my greatsword'll paint the snow red before dawn breaks.

Daegon: That cloaked shadow by the monolith looks like one of those silent Greybeards plotting their next breath of wisdom—bet I could fireball the frost right off his robes and see if he shouts back. This aurora's got me thinking of my old coven rituals under similar lights, twisting shadows into something deadly fun. Jenassa: The aurora paints these peaks like blood on snow, perfect for a midnight stalk; that figure's stance screams vulnerability, one swift draw from my iron sword and it'd be done. Hate how the cold clings to my pauldrons, but it steels the kill—better than those earthen tombs any day. Lydia: Dibella's aurora blesses this frozen vigil, turning High Hrothgar's stones into a warrior's throne under the stars; that cloaked fool by the monolith better not be some Thalmor spy, or my ebony greatsword'll end him quick, honoring our Nord blood against foreign taint. These pharaoh trappings feel odd in the snow, but they don't dull my edge for guarding against those bandit whores.

Daegon: Whirlwind Sprint? That shout just whipped the snow into a frenzy like a coven whirlwind—feels like the aurora's echoing it back, turning this gated path into a slippery trap for anyone dumb enough to follow. Bet those bandit sluts are freezing their asses off below, plotting their next failed ambush. Jenassa: The shout's gust scatters the frost like fleeing prey;

Jenassa: Another cat down to my iron blades, and that wolf's hide would fetch a fair price in Riften's shadows—these autumn ferns rustle with more threats, like those bandits eyeing us from the path's bend, and the deer scattering under the orange glow.
